mnemonics: STRUCTURE: Single Type, Reusable Under One Name. UNION: One Name, Lots Of Interpretations, Now.
keyPoints: A structure (struct) groups variables of different data types under a single name, with each member having its own memory.
A union groups variables of different data types under a single name, but all members share the same memory location.
Only one member of a union can contain a value at any given time; storing a value in one member overwrites the data in the others.
commonMistakes: Treating a union like a struct and attempting to use multiple members simultaneously, which corrupts the data.
Forgetting that the size of a union is the size of its largest member, while a struct's size is the sum of its members (plus potential padding).
Using the wrong member to access the data stored in a union, leading to incorrect interpretation of the bits in memory.
